name: HTML Fundamentals
summary: Build semantic pages with modern HTML5 structure.

lessons.title: HTML Basics
lessons.content: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the skeleton of every website. It isn't a programming language; it is a markup language that tells the browser how to structure the content you see, such as headings, paragraphs, and images. HTML5 is the latest version, introducing new semantic elements like <header>, <nav>, and <footer> to improve accessibility and SEO.

lessons.title: Links and Images
lessons.content: Links connect pages and images make content visual. Use href for links and src for images.

lessons.title: Forms and Inputs
lessons.content: Forms collect user data. Use input types like text, email, and submit for different fields.
lessons.code:
<form action="/submit" method="post">
  <label for="name">Name:</label>
  <input type="text" id="name" name="name" />
  <input type="submit" value="Submit" />
</form>

lessons.title: Semantic HTML
lessons.content: Semantic tags improve readability and SEO. Use <article>, <section>, and <aside> to structure content meaningfully.
lessons.code:
Article Title

Article content goes here.

Author: John Doe

lessons.title: HTML5 APIs
lessons.content: HTML5 includes APIs for multimedia, geolocation, and offline storage. Use the video tag for embedding videos and the canvas tag for drawing graphics.
lessons.code:
<video controls>
  <source src="movie.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser does not support the video tag.
</video>

<canvas id="myCanvas" width="200" height="100">
  Your browser does not support the canvas element.
</canvas>

name: CSS
summary: Style layouts with Flexbox, Grid, and responsive design.

lessons.title: CSS Introduction
lessons.content: CSS is the language we use to style an HTML document. It describes how elements should be displayed.

lessons.title: Selectors
lessons.content: Selectors are used to target HTML elements. You can select by tag name, class (.), or ID (#).

lessons.title: Colors & Backgrounds
lessons.content: Colors can be specified by names, RGB, HEX, or HSL values.

lessons.title: The Box Model
lessons.content: Every element is a box. The box model consists of Margins, Borders, Padding, and the actual Content.

lessons.title: Text Styling
lessons.content: Use properties like font-family, font-size, and text-align to style your typography.

lessons.title: Display Property
lessons.content: The display property specifies if/how an element is displayed (block, inline, inline-block, none).

lessons.title: Positioning
lessons.content: The position property specifies the type of positioning used (static, relative, fixed, absolute, sticky).

lessons.title: Flexbox
lessons.content: Flexbox makes it easier to design flexible responsive layout structures without using floats.

lessons.title: CSS Grid
lessons.content: Grid Layout offers a grid-based layout system, with rows and columns.

lessons.title: Media Queries
lessons.content: Media queries are the most popular method for creating responsive web designs.

name: JavaScript
summary: Learn variables, functions, arrays, and DOM manipulation.

lessons.title: JS Introduction
lessons.content: JavaScript is the world's most popular programming language. It is the language of the Web.

lessons.title: Variables
lessons.content: Variables are containers for storing data values. Use let or const.

lessons.title: Data Types
lessons.content: JS variables can hold different data types: numbers, strings, objects and more.
lessons.code:
let x = 16; // Number
let name = 'Johnson'; // String

lessons.title: Functions
lessons.content: A function is a block of code designed to perform a particular task.

lessons.title: Objects
lessons.content: Objects are variables too. But objects can contain many values.

lessons.title: Events
lessons.content: HTML events are 'things' that happen to HTML elements, like a click or a hover.

lessons.title: Arrays
lessons.content: Arrays are used to store multiple values in a single variable.

lessons.title: Conditionals
lessons.content: Use if/else to perform different actions based on different conditions.

lessons.title: Loops
lessons.content: Loops are handy if you want to run the same code over and over again.

lessons.title: DOM Intro
lessons.content: With the HTML DOM, JavaScript can access and change all the elements of an HTML document.

name: React Js
summary: Build component-based UIs with hooks and routing.

lessons.title: React Introduction
lessons.content: React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces. It is used to build single-page applications and allows us to create reusable UI components.

lessons.title: JSX Fundamentals
lessons.content: JSX stands for JavaScript XML. It allows us to write HTML in React and place them in the DOM without any createElement() methods.

lessons.title: Components (Function vs Class)
lessons.content: Components are like functions that return HTML elements. They are independent and reusable bits of code. Today, Function components are the standard.

lessons.title: Props (Properties)
lessons.content: Props are arguments passed into React components. They are passed via HTML attributes and are read-only.

lessons.title: React useState Hook
lessons.content: The useState hook allows us to track state in a function component. State generally refers to data or properties that need to be tracked in an application.

lessons.title: React useEffect Hook
lessons.content: The useEffect hook allows you to perform side effects in your components, such as fetching data, directly updating the DOM, and timers.

lessons.title: Handling Events
lessons.content: React events are written in camelCase syntax (onClick instead of onclick). Event handlers are passed as functions rather than strings.

lessons.title: Conditional Rendering
lessons.content: In React, you can create distinct components that encapsulate the behavior you need. Then, you can render only some of them, depending on the state.

lessons.title: Lists and Keys
lessons.content: You can build collections of elements and include them in JSX using curly braces. Keys help React identify which items have changed, are added, or are removed.

lessons.title: React Router
lessons.content: React Router is the standard routing library for React. It allows the navigation among views of various components in a React Application.

name: Node.js Essentials
summary: Master server-side JavaScript to build scalable network applications and REST APIs.

lessons.title: What is Node.js?
lessons.content: Node.js is an open-source, cross-platform, JavaScript runtime environment that executes JavaScript code outside a web browser. It is built on Chrome's V8 engine and uses an event-driven, non-blocking I/O model.
lessons.code:
// Check your version in terminal:
// node -v

lessons.title: Node.js Modules
lessons.content: Consider modules to be the same as JavaScript libraries. Node.js has a set of built-in modules which you can use without any further installation (like 'http', 'fs', and 'path').

lessons.title: NPM (Node Package Manager)
lessons.content: NPM is the world's largest software registry. It is installed automatically with Node.js. Use it to install packages like Express, Mongoose, or Axios.

lessons.title: File System Module
lessons.content: The Node.js file system module allows you to work with the file system on your computer. Common tasks include reading, creating, updating, and deleting files.

lessons.title: URL Module
lessons.content: The URL module splits a web address into readable parts. This is essential for routing and handling different request parameters.

lessons.title: Events & EventEmitter
lessons.content: Every action on a computer is an event. Node.js has a built-in module, called 'Events', where you can create-, fire-, and listen for your own events.

lessons.title: Introduction to Express
lessons.content: Express is a minimal and flexible Node.js web application framework that provides a robust set of features for web and mobile applications.

lessons.title: REST API Routing
lessons.content: Routing refers to determining how an application responds to a client request to a particular endpoint, which is a URI (or path) and a specific HTTP request method (GET, POST, etc.).

lessons.title: Middleware
lessons.content: Middleware functions are functions that have access to the request object (req), the response object (res), and the next middleware function in the application’s request-response cycle.

lessons.title: Database Connection (MongoDB)
lessons.content: Node.js can be used in database applications. One of the most popular databases for Node.js is MongoDB, a NoSQL database that stores data in JSON-like documents.
